title: Justice Is God's Work
primary_topics: love and justice in God's nature
sub_topics: Ameurfina Melencio Herrera as Philja Chancellor
Bukas Loob sa Diyos (BLD) Catholic Charismatic Community
Marriage encounter / Life in the Spirit seminar
Isaiah 55:8-9 ('My thoughts are not your thoughts')
Romans 8:28 ('God makes all things work for good')
Christ's death as atonement (justice + love reconciled)

stances.claim: The rendition of justice is 'not only a human necessity; it is a divine essentiality' — when we give people their due, impose penalties, uphold liberty, enforce obligations 'fairly and speedily, without fear or favor — we really do the work of the Lord.' To dispense *quality* justice is to do God's work.

Justice is in the nature of God — He, 'even if He is the most powerful Being in the universe, cannot refuse to impose the sanction of His own law' without violating His own nature. Just as it is in His nature to love, it is also in His essence to see that justice is done.
stances.rhetorical_move: ontological-divine framing (justice + love as inseparable divine essences)

stances.claim: Through Christ's incarnation, suffering, crucifixion, death, and resurrection, God 'atoned for our sins and owned the punishment meant for us' — by sending His only Son He saved humankind 'from eternal damnation. In this manner, His justice and His love were both fulfilled.'

From 1986 to 1995 he and Leni 'underwent a spiritual rebirth' as they joined BLD (Bukas Loob sa Diyos), a charismatic community of renewed Catholics — through marriage encounter, Life in the Spirit seminar, retreats, Bible study, and eventually becoming servant-leaders, they had 'a most fulfilling encounter with our Lord Jesus Christ.'
stances.rhetorical_move: structured-spiritual-journey biographical narrative (recurring CJ set piece)

stances.claim: He has learned to trust God and depend on Him for *all* needs, aspirations, wants — 'even if I do not get what I want, still, I continue to repose my full faith and confidence in Him… He will provide all things good for me and my family — in His own time and in His own way.'
stances.rhetorical_move: providential-trust framing + 'His own time, His own way' aphorism (echoes column C17)

stances.claim: When baffled by events he does not understand, he remembers Isaiah 55:8-9: 'My thoughts are not your thoughts, And my ways are not your ways… As high as the heavens are above the earth, So high are my ways above your ways.'
stances.rhetorical_move: verbatim Isaiah quotation as paradoxical-providence framework

institutions: Bukas Loob sa Diyos Catholic Charismatic Community

signature_phrases: to dispense quality justice is to do God's work
in His own time and in His own way
the rendition of justice is not only a human necessity; it is a divine essentiality
the men and women in black robes
fairly and speedily, without fear or favor
His Honor is our Divine Judge and we are His unworthy children
My thoughts are not your thoughts, And my ways are not your ways
God makes all things work for the good of those who love Him
Justice was served — and so was love
register_markers: biblical verbatim quotations (Isaiah, Romans, Matthew, Mark)
decision_framework_signals: applies ontological-divine framework (justice + love as twin inseparable essences of God) for theological-juridical synthesis
uses Christ's atonement as soteriological-resolution of justice-love tension
frames quality-justice dispensation as divine work, elevating judicial vocation to sacred status
uses 'in His own time, His own way' as paradoxical-providence framework for unanswered prayer
applies Isaiah 55:8-9 framing for accepting events one does not understand
uses judge-pays-daughter's-fine parable as accessible theology of substitutionary atonement
notable_anecdotes: Quoting his own February 1, 1999 tribute to retiring Justice Martinez: 'He has definitely left his unmistakable imprint in my memory of great jurists'
BLD (Bukas Loob sa Diyos) journey 1986-1995 with Leni — marriage encounter, Life in the Spirit seminar, becoming servant-leaders

The traffic-court parable: judge fining his own daughter P300, then paying her fine out of his own wallet at the cashier — 'Justice was served — and so was love' — used as accessible analogy for Christ's atonement
